title: FamilyRelationships
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. We then define:

  1. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
  2. The set of parents of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
  3. The set of children of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
  4. The set of children of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
  5. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
  6. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
  7. The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
  8. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
  9. The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
  10. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
  11. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
  12. The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
  13. The district of $v$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
  14. The district of $A$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_5 -- "FamilyRelationships".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_5
% title: FamilyRelationships
\def\rowref{def\_3\_5}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_5}

\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and the walk definitions of def \ref{def:walks}. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$ and let $A \ins J \cup V$ be a subset of nodes. We adopt the convention that each individual form $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ defined below is defined for any $v \in J \cup V$ (not only $v \in V$); in particular, input nodes $v \in J$ admit the same set-builder definitions, and each set form $\Pa^G(A)$, $\Ch^G(A)$, $\Anc^G(A)$, $\Desc^G(A)$, $\Sc^G(A)$, $\Dist^G(A)$ is well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because every summand $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ is defined.

    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{Parents.}
            The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Pa^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (w, v) \in E \rC.
            \]
            The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).
            \]

        \item \emph{Children.}
            The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Ch^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in E \rC.
            \]
            The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Ch^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).
            \]

        \item \emph{Siblings.}
            The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Sib^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in L \rC.
            \]

        \item \emph{Ancestors.}
            The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Anc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } w \text{ to } v \text{ in } G \rC,
            \]
            where ``directed walk'' is in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. In particular, the length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ (admitted by def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., as the single-vertex tuple $(v)$ with no edges) witnesses $v \in \Anc^G(v)$. Hence
            \[
                v \in \Anc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally}: this holds regardless of whether any edge (in particular any directed self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

            The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Anc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

        \item \emph{Descendants.}
            The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Desc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC,
            \]
            with ``directed walk'' as in def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, so
            \[
                v \in \Desc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally} (regardless of whether any edge, in particular any directed self-loop, incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

            The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Desc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

        \item \emph{Non-descendants.}
            The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).
            \]

        \item \emph{Strongly connected component.}
            The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).
            \]
            Since $v \in \Anc^G(v)$ and $v \in \Desc^G(v)$ unconditionally (items~iv.\ and~v.), it follows that
            \[
                v \in \Sc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally} (regardless of whether any edge incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

            The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

        \item \emph{District.}
            The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a bidirected walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC,
            \]
            where ``bidirected walk'' is in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~iii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The lecture notes' displayed shape ``$v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w$'' is to be read here purely as syntactic sugar for ``walk all of whose edges lie in $L$''; the intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}$ imposes no lower bound on $n$, and in particular admits $n = 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) bidirected walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so
            \[
                v \in \Dist^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally}: neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required for self-membership (and indeed a bidirected self-loop at $v$ is excluded a priori by the irreflexivity of $L$ in def \ref{def-cdmg}).

            The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$ unconditionally.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the walk definitions of def def:walks. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$ and let $A \ins J \cup V$ be a subset of nodes. We adopt the convention that each individual form $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ defined below is defined for any $v \in J \cup V$ (not only $v \in V$); in particular, input nodes $v \in J$ admit the same set-builder definitions, and each set form $\Pa^G(A)$, $\Ch^G(A)$, $\Anc^G(A)$, $\Desc^G(A)$, $\Sc^G(A)$, $\Dist^G(A)$ is well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because every summand $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ is defined.

  1. Parents. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Pa^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (w, v) \in E \rC. \] The set of parents of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v). \]
  2. Children. The set of children of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Ch^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in E \rC. \] The set of children of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Ch^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v). \]
  3. Siblings. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Sib^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in L \rC. \]
  4. Ancestors. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Anc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } w \text{ to } v \text{ in } G \rC, \] where ŌĆ£directed walkŌĆØ is in the sense of def def:walks, item ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. In particular, the length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ (admitted by def def:walks, item ii., as the single-vertex tuple $(v)$ with no edges) witnesses $v \in \Anc^G(v)$. Hence \[ v \in \Anc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally: this holds regardless of whether any edge (in particular any directed self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

    The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Anc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

  5. Descendants. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Desc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC, \] with ŌĆ£directed walkŌĆØ as in def def:walks, item ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, so \[ v \in \Desc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally (regardless of whether any edge, in particular any directed self-loop, incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

    The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Desc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

  6. Non-descendants. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A). \]
  7. Strongly connected component. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v). \] Since $v \in \Anc^G(v)$ and $v \in \Desc^G(v)$ unconditionally (items iv.\ and v.), it follows that \[ v \in \Sc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally (regardless of whether any edge incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

    The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

  8. District. The district of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a bidirected walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC, \] where ŌĆ£bidirected walkŌĆØ is in the sense of def def:walks, item iii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The lecture notes' displayed shape ŌĆ£$v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w$ŌĆØ is to be read here purely as syntactic sugar for ŌĆ£walk all of whose edges lie in $L$ŌĆØ; the intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}$ imposes no lower bound on $n$, and in particular admits $n = 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) bidirected walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so \[ v \in \Dist^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally: neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required for self-membership (and indeed a bidirected self-loop at $v$ is excluded a priori by the irreflexivity of $L$ in def def-cdmg).

    The district of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$ unconditionally.

lean_blocks.explanation: This helper introduces section-wide variables shared by every definition that follows. The Lean `variable` command does not create a new declaration; it tells the elaborator that any later `def` mentioning `Node` should auto-insert these binders. The braces around `{Node : Type*}` make `Node` an *implicit* argument: callers write `Pa G v` and Lean infers `Node` from `G`'s type. `Type*` is Mathlib shorthand for a universe-polymorphic `Type u` ŌĆö useful because nothing in this row forces a concrete universe level. The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance argument provides decidable equality on the carrier, needed by `Finset` operations (the CDMG stores edges in `Finset (Node ├Ś Node)`). Keeping this variable block here means the family operators inherit the same instance without each `def` restating it.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide implicit type variable and decidability instance shared by every def below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def Pa (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (w, v) Ōłł G.E}
lean_blocks.explanation: `Pa G v` is the set of *parents* of a vertex `v`: every node `w` with a directed edge `w ŌåÆ v` in `G`. The return type `Set Node` is Mathlib's predicate-based set (a function `Node ŌåÆ Prop`), chosen uniformly for the whole row so walk-based operators (`Anc`, `Desc`, `Dist`) ŌĆö which would otherwise need a decidability proof for `Finset` ŌĆö share the same carrier. The body uses set-builder notation `{w | ŌĆ”}`. The conjunct `w Ōłł G` invokes the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `CDMGNotation.lean` and unfolds to `w Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V`. It is technically redundant ŌĆö the CDMG axiom `hE_subset` already pins edge endpoints inside `J Ōł¬ V` ŌĆö but kept to mirror the LN tex character-for-character and let consumers destructure `Ō¤©hw_mem, hw_edgeŌ¤®` immediately.

lean_blocks.code:
def PaSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Pa v
lean_blocks.explanation: `PaSet G A` lifts the per-vertex parent operator to a *set* of vertices by taking the indexed union of `Pa G v` as `v` ranges over `A`. The notation `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Pa v` is Mathlib's bounded indexed union (`Set.biUnion`); it unfolds to `{w | Ōłā v Ōłł A, w Ōłł G.Pa v}`. Defining the set form as a `biUnion` (rather than a custom set-builder) gives free access to Mathlib's algebraic API: `Set.biUnion_union` proves `PaSet G (A Ōł¬ B) = PaSet G A Ōł¬ PaSet G B`, `Set.biUnion_mono` gives monotonicity, and `Set.biUnion_singleton` gives `PaSet G {v} = Pa G v` ŌĆö the singleton identity the LN deliberately preserves by not excluding `v` from `Pa G v` when a self-loop is present. `A : Set Node` (not `Finset`) keeps the definition compatible with infinite ambient types.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Parents of a set `A`: indexed union of per-vertex parent sets, matching the LN.
def PaSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, ŌĆ”` is `Set.biUnion`; gives `biUnion_union`, `biUnion_mono`, etc. for free.
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Pa v

lean_blocks.code:
def Ch (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (v, w) Ōłł G.E}
lean_blocks.explanation: `Ch G v` is the mirror of `Pa G v` with the edge direction flipped: `w` is a *child* of `v` iff there is a directed edge `v ŌåÆ w`. The shape of the body is identical to `Pa` modulo swapping `(w, v)` for `(v, w)` in the membership check on `G.E` (a `Finset (Node ├Ś Node)`). The redundant `w Ōłł G` conjunct is kept for the same two reasons as in `Pa`: LN-tex parity and immediate pair-destructuring at use sites. Note the self-loop convention: if `(v, v) Ōłł G.E`, then `v Ōłł Ch G v` ŌĆö the LN does not comment on this case explicitly for children (only for parents) but we follow the symmetric literal definition so the singleton identity `ChSet G {v} = Ch G v` (via `Set.biUnion_singleton`) holds. Patching out `v` would break that identity.

lean_blocks.code:
def ChSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Ch v
lean_blocks.explanation: `ChSet G A` is the set form of children: the indexed union `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Ch G v`. Same `Set.biUnion` shape as `PaSet`, so the same Mathlib API lifts: `Set.biUnion_union` for the additive identity over `A`, `Set.biUnion_mono` for monotonicity in `A`, `Set.biUnion_singleton` for `ChSet G {v} = Ch G v`. The singleton identity is the load-bearing reason the per-vertex `Ch G v` retains `v` itself when a self-loop is present (see the design note on `Ch`). Picking `A : Set Node` instead of `Finset Node` keeps the definition general and lets downstream consumers that work with finite sets pass `(A : Set Node)` via the `Finset ŌåÆ Set` coercion without losing access to the rich `Set` lemma library Mathlib provides.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Children of a set `A`: indexed union, same biUnion shape as `PaSet`.
def ChSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- `Set.biUnion_singleton` then gives the singleton identity `ChSet G {v} = Ch G v`.
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Ch v

lean_blocks.code:
def Sib (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ s(v, w) Ōłł G.L}
lean_blocks.explanation: `Sib G v` is the set of *siblings* of `v`: nodes `w` connected to `v` by a bidirected edge in `G`. The body shape mirrors `Pa`/`Ch` ŌĆö set-builder `{w | ŌĆ”}`, redundant-but-kept `w Ōłł G` conjunct for LN parity ŌĆö with one structural shift: the channel test reads `s(v, w) Ōłł G.L` rather than `(v, w) Ōłł G.L`. The notation `s(v, w)` is Mathlib's `Sym2` quotient builder (`Sym2.mk` on the ordered pair, identifying `s(v, w) = s(w, v)`), reflecting that `G.L` has carrier `Finset (Sym2 Node)`: bidirected edges are unordered pairs by type. Symmetry `w Ōłł Sib G v Ōåö v Ōłł Sib G w` is then a definitional rewrite via `Sym2.eq_swap`, no `hL_symm` hypothesis needed. The LN's def block uniquely omits a set form for siblings, so `Sib` has no `SibSet` twin. Self-membership fails: `s(v, v).IsDiag` is `True`, so `hL_irrefl : s Ōłł G.L ŌåÆ ┬¼ s.IsDiag` forces `s(v, v) Ōłē G.L` and `v Ōłē Sib G v`.

lean_blocks.code:
def Anc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā p : Walk G w v, p.IsDirectedWalk}
lean_blocks.explanation: `Anc G v` is the set of *ancestors* of `v`: nodes `w` from which a directed walk reaches `v`. The encoding leans on `def_3_4`'s `Walk G w v` type ŌĆö an inductive carrying the walk as data ŌĆö paired with the predicate `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` which asserts that every edge along the walk is directed. A `Relation.TransGen`-style transitive closure was considered and rejected: it loses the walk-as-data needed downstream for path extraction and m-separation arguments. The existential `Ōłā p : Walk G w v, p.IsDirectedWalk` admits *any* length, crucially including length 0 (`Walk.nil`), so the LN's note `v Ōłł Anc^G(v)` is unconditional ŌĆö witnessed by `Ō¤©Walk.nil v hv, trivialŌ¤®` regardless of whether a directed self-loop at `v` exists. The `w Ōłł G` conjunct is redundant for nontrivial walks but needed in the `Walk.nil` case.

lean_blocks.code:
def AncSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Anc v
lean_blocks.explanation: `AncSet G A` is the indexed union `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Anc G v` ŌĆö the set form of ancestors. Same `Set.biUnion` shape as the other set forms in this row. The LN's note `A ŌŖå Anc^G(A)` falls out as a one-line corollary of `Anc`'s unconditional self-membership: for any `v Ōłł A` we have `v Ōłł Anc G v` (via `Walk.nil`) and `Anc G v ŌŖå AncSet G A` (via `Set.subset_biUnion_of_mem`), so `v Ōłł AncSet G A`. No extra axiom or patch is needed; the inclusion is purely a consequence of the chosen primitive. Algebraic identities like `AncSet G (A Ōł¬ B) = AncSet G A Ōł¬ AncSet G B` lift directly from Mathlib's `Set.biUnion_union`, and `AncSet G {v} = Anc G v` from `Set.biUnion_singleton`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Ancestors of a set `A`: indexed union, same biUnion shape as other set forms.
def AncSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- LN's `A ŌŖå AncSet G A` is a one-line corollary of `v Ōłł Anc G v` (via `Walk.nil`).
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Anc v

lean_blocks.code:
def Desc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā p : Walk G v w, p.IsDirectedWalk}
lean_blocks.explanation: `Desc G v` is the mirror of `Anc G v` with the walk direction reversed: a node `w` is a *descendant* of `v` iff there is a directed walk from `v` to `w` ŌĆö i.e. the existential is on `Walk G v w` (start at `v`, end at the candidate `w`) instead of `Walk G w v`. The same `IsDirectedWalk` predicate from `def_3_4` is reused, so every downstream lemma about directed walks lifts to both `Anc` and `Desc` after a walk-reversal step. Self-membership `v Ōłł Desc G v` is again unconditional, witnessed by `Ō¤©Walk.nil v hv, trivialŌ¤®` ŌĆö the length-0 walk satisfies `IsDirectedWalk` vacuously. `Desc G v` is the natural carrier for do-calculus' post-intervention support and is intersected with `NonDesc G A` downstream, so the uniform `Set Node` return type avoids `Finset.coe` round-trips at those use sites.

lean_blocks.code:
def DescSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Desc v
lean_blocks.explanation: `DescSet G A` is the indexed union `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Desc G v`. Beyond the usual algebraic identities (additivity in `A`, monotonicity, singleton collapse via `Set.biUnion_singleton`), this set form is load-bearing for `NonDesc` below: the LN defines non-descendants as `(J Ōł¬ V) \ DescSet G A`, using the *set form*, not a per-vertex `Desc`. Keeping `DescSet` as a clean `biUnion` lets `NonDesc` decompositions reduce via Mathlib's `Set.compl_biUnion` / `Set.diff_biUnion` rather than a custom walk-quantifier lemma. The LN's `A ŌŖå Desc^G(A)` note follows from `Desc`'s self-membership combined with `Set.subset_biUnion_of_mem`, same pattern as `AncSet`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Descendants of a set `A`: indexed union; load-bearing for `NonDesc` below.
def DescSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- BiUnion shape lets `NonDesc` reduce via `Set.compl_biUnion` / `Set.diff_biUnion`.
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Desc v

lean_blocks.code:
def NonDesc (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  ((G.J Ōł¬ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node) \ G.DescSet A
lean_blocks.explanation: `NonDesc G A` is the set of nodes inside the ambient vertex set `J Ōł¬ V` that are *not* descendants of `A`. Three subtle choices: (1) the complement is taken inside `G.J Ōł¬ G.V` (the CDMG's vertex set), not `Set.univ` ŌĆö otherwise every term of the ambient `Node : Type*` would count as a non-descendant, which downstream chapters on local Markov and do-calculus rule 2 cannot use. (2) The `((G.J Ōł¬ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node)` coercion lifts the `Finset` union to a `Set` via `Finset.coe_union`, so `Finset` lemmas about `J Ōł¬ V` apply without unfolding the `Membership` instance. (3) The LN has no per-vertex `NonDesc G v` form, so we do not introduce one; consumers wanting it write `NonDesc G {v}` explicitly. The `\` operator is `Set.diff`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Non-descendants of `A`: complement of `DescSet G A` inside the ambient `J Ōł¬ V`.
def NonDesc (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- Coerce the `Finset` union to `Set`; complement inside `J Ōł¬ V`, NOT `Set.univ`.
  -- `\` is `Set.diff`. The LN has no per-vertex form, so neither do we.
  ((G.J Ōł¬ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node) \ G.DescSet A

lean_blocks.code:
def Sc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  G.Anc v Ōł® G.Desc v
lean_blocks.explanation: `Sc G v` is the *strongly connected component* of `v`: the intersection of `Anc G v` and `Desc G v`. The encoding is a character-for-character translation of the LN formula using Mathlib's `Set.inter` (notation `Ōł®`); rolling our own "bidirectional reachability" predicate would force a fresh existential witness pair at every use site instead of reusing the existentials already living inside `Anc` and `Desc`. The LN's self-membership note `v Ōłł Sc G v` is *not* a separate axiom ŌĆö it is an immediate corollary of `v Ōłł Anc G v` and `v Ōłł Desc G v` (both unconditional via `Walk.nil`) combined with `Set.mem_inter`. So all load-bearing self-membership work happens in `Anc`/`Desc`; `Sc` inherits it transparently. Downstream chapters use `Sc` for acyclification, the ID algorithm's component decomposition, and Žā-AMP constructions.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Strongly connected component of `v`: intersection of ancestors and descendants.
def Sc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  -- `Ōł®` is `Set.inter`; `v Ōłł Sc G v` follows from `Anc`/`Desc` self-membership.
  G.Anc v Ōł® G.Desc v

lean_blocks.code:
def ScSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Sc v
lean_blocks.explanation: `ScSet G A` is the indexed union of per-vertex strongly connected components over `v Ōłł A`. The LN's name "(union of) strongly connected components of `A`" explicitly parenthesises "(union of)" ŌĆö `ScSet G A` is genuinely a *union* of components, not a single component of `A`. When `A` straddles multiple SCCs there is no single component containing all of it; downstream chapters that need the partition view (equivalence classes under mutual reachability) construct it locally from `Sc G v` rather than from `ScSet G A`. The same `Set.biUnion` shape and same one-line corollary `A ŌŖå ScSet G A` (from `Sc`'s unconditional self-membership) as the other set forms in this row.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Union of strongly connected components over `A`: NOT a single component when `A` straddles SCCs.
def ScSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- `A ŌŖå ScSet G A` follows from `v Ōłł Sc G v` + `Set.subset_biUnion_of_mem`.
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Sc v

lean_blocks.code:
def Dist (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā p : Walk G v w, p.IsBidirectedWalk}
lean_blocks.explanation: `Dist G v` is the *district* of `v`: nodes `w` reachable from `v` by a walk whose edges are all bidirected. The carrier is again `Walk G v w` from `def_3_4`, but the predicate switches to `IsBidirectedWalk`. The LN's pictorial display `v Ōåö vŌéü Ōåö Ōŗ» Ōåö vŌéÖŌéŗŌéü Ōåö w` is syntactic sugar for "walk all of whose edges lie in `L`" with no lower bound on length; encoding through `Walk + IsBidirectedWalk` sidesteps the indexing ambiguity entirely (`Walk.nil` is length 0, `Walk.cons` adds edges, no index-counting needed). Self-membership `v Ōłł Dist G v` is unconditional via `Ō¤©Walk.nil v hv, trivialŌ¤®` ŌĆö neither a bidirected self-loop at `v` (which `hL_irrefl` forbids anyway) nor a length-2 bidirected cycle through `v` is required. The walk direction `Walk G v w` is chosen to mirror `Desc`; symmetry `w Ōłł Dist G v Ōåö v Ōłł Dist G w` is a one-line corollary of walk reversal plus `hL_symm`.

lean_blocks.code:
def DistSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Dist v
lean_blocks.explanation: `DistSet G A` is the indexed union `Ōŗā v Ōłł A, Dist G v` ŌĆö the final set form in the row. Same `Set.biUnion` shape, same one-line corollary `A ŌŖå DistSet G A` (from `Dist`'s self-membership plus `Set.subset_biUnion_of_mem`), same singleton collapse via `Set.biUnion_singleton`. One subtle caveat: `DistSet G A` is the *union* of per-vertex districts, not "the unique district containing `A`". Some ADMG literature reserves "district of `A`" for the maximal bidirected-connected component containing `A` (when one exists); the two readings agree iff `A` lies in a single component. The LN uses the union convention, and downstream chapters (ID algorithm, c-component decomposition) state the "single component" hypothesis explicitly when needed.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- District of a set `A`: indexed union; NOT "the unique district containing `A`".
def DistSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  -- Agrees with the ADMG "single component" reading iff `A` lies in one component.
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Dist v

addition_to_the_LN:
[self_membership_notes_require_length_zero_walks] In the definitions of $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$, the directed and bidirected walks are understood to include the trivial walk of length $0$ consisting of the single vertex $v$ with no edges. Consequently, $v \in \Anc^G(v)$, $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, $v \in \Sc^G(v)$, and $v \in \Dist^G(v)$ hold for every $v \in V$ unconditionally, regardless of whether any edge (in particular any self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

[type_mismatch_individual_vs_set_versions] The preamble should read "$v, w \in J \cup V$" rather than "$v, w \in V$": the individual definitions $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$ are defined for any $v \in J \cup V$, using the same set-builder expressions given. In particular, input nodes ($v \in J$) may have children, descendants, etc., and the set-version definitions $\Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)$ (and analogously for the other operators) are well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because each summand is defined.

[district_walk_indexing_ambiguous_for_small_n] $w \in \Dist^G(v)$ iff there exists a walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ in which every edge is bidirected ($\huh$). The walk may have any length $\geq 0$; in particular, the length-$0$ walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required. The intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \ldots, v_{n-1}$ in the displayed formula should be read as syntactic sugar for "walk consisting of bidirected edges" and imposes no lower bound on walk length.
design_choices:
**Carrier is `Set Node`, not `Finset Node`, across the whole family.** Three of the operators (`Anc`, `Desc`, `Dist`) are defined by existence of a walk, which is not immediately decidable on a general CDMG (the walk inductive ranges over arbitrary lengths even though the node set is finite). A `Finset` alternative would force a `Decidable` proof for walk-existence at the definition site and thread that instance through every later use. Picking `Set Node` keeps the API uniform: every Boolean-algebra identity ($\Pa(A \cup B) = \Pa(A) \cup \Pa(B)$, $\NonDesc = (J \cup V) \setminus \Desc$, $\Sc = \Anc \cap \Desc$) lands inside Mathlib's `Set` API with no `Finset.coe` round-trips; finiteness is recovered separately as needed via the ambient bound `(G.J Ōł¬ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node`.

**Per-vertex form is the primitive; the set form is a Mathlib `Set.biUnion`.** The LN consistently builds set forms by union over per-vertex forms, so the same layering is mirrored here. Downstream proofs lift the LN's algebraic identities directly via `Set.biUnion_union`, `Set.biUnion_mono`, `Set.biUnion_singleton`, and `Set.mem_biUnion`. Inverting the primitive (defining the set form first and the per-vertex form as `Pa G {v}`) would force every per-vertex use site to peel off a singleton biUnion; a custom recursive fold would re-prove every identity from scratch.

**Per-vertex operators range over `v Ōłł J Ōł¬ V`, not `v Ōłł V`.** The LN's preamble `v, w Ōłł V` is too narrow to type-check the set forms for $A \subseteq J \cup V$; the rewritten spec uniformly admits `v Ōłł J Ōł¬ V`. No `v Ōłł G` hypothesis is carried on the function argument ŌĆö the set-builder body conjoins `w Ōłł G` on the output side, so out-of-graph inputs simply yield the empty set (or a `Walk.nil`-only set for the walk-based operators, since `Walk.nil` itself requires `v Ōłł G`).

**The literal LN set-builder is kept for `Pa`/`Ch`, not the patched `\setminus \{v\}` alternative.** The LN's source explicitly considers and rejects $\Pa^G(v) := \{w \mid w \to v\} \setminus \{v\}$, because patching out `v` would break the singleton identity `PaSet G {v} = Pa G v` whenever a directed self-loop $(v, v) \in E$ is present. The Lean encoding preserves this load-bearing convention; `Ch` inherits the same shape by symmetry.

**The output-side `w Ōłł G` conjunct is redundant but retained.** The CDMG axiom `hE_subset` already forces directed-edge endpoints into `J Ōł¬ V`, and `hL_subset` forces bidirected-edge endpoints into `V`, so `w Ōłł G` is provable from the edge-membership conjunct alone. It is kept because (i) it mirrors the LN tex character-for-character, easing grep parity, and (ii) downstream destructuring `Ō¤©hw_mem, hw_edgeŌ¤®` gets `hw_mem : w Ōłł G` for free without invoking `hE_subset` / `hL_subset`.

**`Sib` has no set form and no `SibSet`.** The LN's def block uniquely omits a set form for siblings, and the asymmetry is mirrored verbatim; adding `SibSet` would silently introduce a definition the LN does not have. `v Ōłē \Sib^G(v)` always ŌĆö but as a consequence of `hL_irrefl : s Ōłł G.L ŌåÆ ┬¼ s.IsDiag` together with `s(v, v).IsDiag = True`, not as a `\setminus \{v\}` patch on the set-builder body.

**`Sib` reads `G.L` as `Finset (Sym2 Node)`; its body uses `s(v, w) Ōłł G.L`.** Bidirected edges live in the symmetric-quotient type `Sym2 Node`, so `s(v, w) = s(w, v)` is a definitional rewrite (via `Sym2.eq_swap`) and graph-theoretic symmetry `w Ōłł Sib G v Ōåö v Ōłł Sib G w` is a one-line corollary with no `hL_symm` hypothesis to discharge. Encoding `L` as `Finset (Node ├Ś Node)` plus a symmetry axiom was rejected at the CDMG level: it admits ordered-pair-only mirror artefacts that the channel-resolving predicates downstream cannot disambiguate. `Sym2` makes the symmetry structural rather than axiomatic.

**Walk-based operators reuse `Walk G ? ?` + an `IsDirectedWalk` / `IsBidirectedWalk` predicate.** A separate `DirectedWalk` / `BidirectedWalk` inductive, or a `Relation.TransGen G.tuh` closure, were considered and rejected: `TransGen` loses the walk-as-data needed for downstream path extraction and m-separation, and duplicate inductives would force re-proving every constructor case. The predicate-on-`Walk` approach lifts every `nil`/`cons` lemma uniformly across `Anc`, `Desc`, `Dist`.

**Self-membership in `Anc`/`Desc`/`Sc`/`Dist` falls out of `Walk.nil`, not a `Ōł¬ {v}` patch.** The LN's unconditional notes $v \in \Anc^G(v)$ etc. are witnessed by `Ō¤©Walk.nil v hv, trivialŌ¤®`, exploiting that `IsDirectedWalk` and `IsBidirectedWalk` return `True` on `nil` vacuously. For `Sc`, self-membership is a pure corollary of `Anc Ōł® Desc` ŌĆö no third witness is added. This makes the LN's "regardless of self-loops" caveat structurally automatic rather than a separate axiom.

**`NonDesc` has no per-vertex form and complements inside `(J Ōł¬ V : Finset)`.** Following the LN literally avoids silently introducing `NonDesc G v` (which would invite confusion with `NonDesc G {v}`, even though they are equal via `Set.biUnion_singleton`). Complementing inside `V` alone would wrongly exclude input nodes; complementing inside `Set.univ` would wrongly include nodes outside the CDMG's vertex set. The `Finset ŌåÆ Set` coercion `((G.J Ōł¬ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node)` is preferred over a manual `{w | w Ōłł G}` set-builder because it lifts `Finset.mem_union` / `Finset.coe_union` lemmas automatically.

**`Sc` is the literal intersection `Anc Ōł® Desc`, not a re-rolled bidirectional-reachability predicate.** Encoding `Sc` directly via `Set.inter` reuses the existentials already inside `Anc` and `Desc` separately, instead of forcing a fresh pair of walk witnesses at every use site, and composes cleanly with `Set.mem_inter_iff` rewriting.

**`Dist` uses `Walk G v w` rather than `Walk G w v`.** Bidirected walks are reversible because `Sym2`'s swap symmetry makes the channel direction-irrelevant on each step; picking `Walk G v w` mirrors `Desc G v`'s shape, making the `Anc`/`Desc`/`Dist` family visually uniform and their downstream lemmas (transitivity, monotonicity, walk reversal) shareable. The expected symmetry $w \in \Dist^G(v) \leftrightarrow v \in \Dist^G(w)$ is then a one-line corollary chaining walk reversal with `Sym2.eq_swap`. The LN's pictorial indexing $v \leftrightarrow v_1 \leftrightarrow \cdots \leftrightarrow v_{n-1} \leftrightarrow w$ is sidestepped entirely by the inductive `Walk.nil` / `Walk.cons` shape, dissolving the small-$n$ indexing ambiguity flagged by the LN addition.
